summary: The Equal Rights Amendment was a major focus of my political efforts, especially during the early 1970s. Despite initial optimism, it faced strong opposition from preachers and women who lobbied against it, which complicated its passage. The Democratic leadership, including Ramsey and Watkins, tried to give legislators an 'out' by pushing a referendum bill instead of a direct vote. Ultimately, political maneuvering and fear of challenging established power led to its defeat in the North Carolina legislature.

Ramsey was part of the Democratic leadership during the ERA debates in North Carolina. Along with Watkins, he requested Tom Sawyer of Greensboro to introduce a referendum bill as a way to give legislators an 'out' from directly voting on the ERA. This move was more about political convenience than genuine support for the amendment. Ramsey's role was pivotal in shaping the legislative strategy that ultimately hindered the ERA's progress.

Watkins was another key figure in the Democratic leadership alongside Ramsey during the ERA fight. He was involved in the strategy to have Tom Sawyer introduce a referendum bill to avoid a direct vote on the ERA. This tactic was intended to provide legislators with a political escape rather than to advance the amendment. Watkins' involvement reflects the complex political dynamics that contributed to the ERA's defeat in the state.

Tom Sawyer, a politician from Greensboro, was asked by Ramsey and Watkins to introduce a referendum bill concerning the ERA. This bill was not meant to genuinely let the public vote on the amendment but rather to give legislators an excuse to avoid a direct vote. Sawyer's role was instrumental in the legislative maneuvering that delayed and complicated the ERA's progress. His involvement highlights the political strategies used by the Democratic leadership at the time.

Greensboro was significant as the home of Tom Sawyer, who played a role in the ERA legislative process. It was also mentioned in relation to Charlie Phillips, who was from Greensboro and was critical of the legislative tactics used against the ERA. The city served as a backdrop for some of the political figures involved in the ERA fight. Greensboro's political community was actively engaged in these debates during the early 1970s.

California was referenced as a contrast to North Carolina regarding the use of referendums. Unlike North Carolina, California allowed referendums on significant state issues like coastal matters. This comparison was used to highlight the insincerity of the referendum bill introduced in North Carolina for the ERA, which was more about giving legislators an escape than genuinely letting the people vote. California's political processes served as a benchmark in this discussion.

The Women's Political Caucus was deeply involved in the ERA campaign in North Carolina. We actually formed and funded the ERA Ratification Council, which included many significant women's organizations. The Caucus worked tirelessly to educate and mobilize women and to lobby legislators, showing our commitment to advancing women's rights. Our efforts, alongside other groups, were crucial even though the ERA ultimately faced defeat.

The ERA Ratification Council was created and funded by the Women's Political Caucus to unite various women's organizations in support of the amendment. It included groups like the Federation of Women's Clubs, League of Women Voters, AAUW, United Church Women, and Business and Professional Women. The Council worked collectively to educate members and lobby the legislature, showing a broad coalition of support for the ERA in North Carolina. Our coordinated efforts were a significant part of the campaign.

The General Federation of Women's Clubs was one of the major women's organizations involved in the ERA Ratification Council. They participated actively in educating their members about the Equal Rights Amendment and supported lobbying efforts. Their involvement helped build a broad base of support among women across North Carolina. This organization was part of the collective push to advance women's rights during the early 1970s.

The League of Women Voters was a key participant in the ERA Ratification Council, working alongside other women's groups to support the amendment. They helped educate their membership and were involved in lobbying efforts aimed at the legislature. Their longstanding commitment to political engagement made them a valuable ally in the ERA campaign. Together with other organizations, they formed a strong coalition advocating for women's equality.

The American Association of University Women (AAUW) was an important member of the ERA Ratification Council. They contributed to educating their members about the ERA and supported efforts to influence the legislature. Their involvement reflected the engagement of educated women in the fight for equal rights. AAUW's participation helped strengthen the coalition pushing for the amendment in North Carolina.

United Church Women were among the significant women's organizations in the ERA Ratification Council. They joined other groups in educating their members and advocating for the amendment. Their participation showed the broad religious and community support for women's rights at the time. The coalition was diverse, including secular and faith-based organizations working together for the ERA.

The Business and Professional Women (BPW) were longstanding supporters of the ERA, having educated their membership on the amendment for fifteen to twenty years. They were active members of the ERA Ratification Council and played a big role in lobbying the legislature. Their professional perspective added weight to the campaign for equal rights. BPW's involvement was crucial in sustaining the momentum for the ERA in North Carolina.

Susie Sharp was a judge from Reidsville whose opinion influenced Kitch Josey, the chairman of the house committee on the ERA referendum bill. She made discreet telephone calls that reportedly swayed his stance against the bill. Her involvement was significant enough to be mentioned as a key factor in the legislative process. Susie Sharp's influence exemplifies how respected individuals could impact political outcomes behind the scenes.

Kitch Josey was the chairman of the house committee responsible for the ERA referendum bill. He was against the bill, a position reportedly influenced by Judge Susie Sharp. After suffering heart attacks, his inability to hold a committee meeting was used as a political maneuver to delay the bill's progress. Josey's role was central to the subterfuge that ultimately prevented the ERA from advancing in the house.

Reidsville is notable as the hometown of Judge Susie Sharp, who played a discreet but influential role in the ERA legislative process. It is also mentioned as a place where Kitch Josey had lived before moving east. The connection between these two figures from Reidsville highlights the local networks that affected state politics. Reidsville thus figures indirectly in the story of the ERA's defeat in North Carolina.

Charlie Phillips, from Greensboro, was critical of the legislative tactics used to block the ERA referendum bill. He believed that the excuse of Kitch Josey's illness should have been challenged and that the committee meeting should have proceeded regardless. Phillips' perspective reflects the frustration of more experienced legislators with the political gamesmanship surrounding the ERA. His comments highlight the internal disagreements within the legislature.

John Sanders was among those who reminded us that it was the legislature's duty to vote on the ERA regardless of public referendums. He acknowledged that the referendum was a political maneuver rather than a genuine attempt to let the people decide. Sanders' comments helped clarify the legislative responsibilities and the political context of the ERA fight. His role was part of the broader dialogue about the amendment's legislative process.

H. L. Mencken was quoted to describe the North Carolina senate during the ERA debates as 'a Sahara,' meaning it was devoid of leadership and character. This harsh characterization reflected the frustration with the senate's opposition and poor conduct during that legislative session. Mencken's words helped capture the bleak political atmosphere we faced in the senate. His perspective was used to emphasize the challenges in getting the ERA passed.

Pat Taylor was someone I contacted to help influence other legislators during the ERA campaign. For example, I asked Pat Taylor to call Hightower as part of our lobbying efforts. Taylor's involvement shows the network of political contacts we leveraged to support the amendment. His role was part of the broader strategy to sway votes in the legislature.

Hightower was a legislator whom we tried to influence through calls and lobbying efforts during the ERA fight. Pat Taylor was asked to reach out to Hightower to help secure support for the amendment. Our efforts to engage Hightower were part of a larger campaign to build legislative backing. Hightower's position was important enough to warrant direct political outreach.

Mike Mullins was one of the legislators who backed out at the last minute on the ERA vote, which was a disappointment given the hard work we had done. His last-minute withdrawal was part of the challenges we faced in securing consistent support. Mullins' change of position exemplifies the uncertainty and political pressure surrounding the amendment. His actions affected the final outcome in the legislature.

Gordon Allen was another legislator who backed out at the last minute on the ERA vote, similar to Mike Mullins. This was frustrating because we had invested significant time and resources lobbying members like him. Allen's withdrawal contributed to the defeat of the amendment in the legislature. His decision reflects the difficult political environment and the challenges of maintaining support for the ERA.
